comments:
Summary:
The paper describes ELPsolve, a new solver for Epistemic Logic Programs (ELPs) that implements an updated semantics (per Shen & Eiter) and a staged, level-ordered, partitioned search strategy to avoid the large memory blow-up of previous generate-and-test approaches. The authors present sequential and parallel variants, a bitvector representation of guesses, an ASP translation, correctness arguments (including an appendix relating the semantics to Shen & Eiter), and experimental results comparing ELPsolve to ELPS on two families of benchmarks (eligibleN and yaleN). Results show substantial runtime and memory improvements, and the solver supports early termination and multiprocessing.

Strengths:
- Addresses an important practical challenge for ELP solving (exponential memory blow-up) with a clear algorithmic approach (partitioning, level-ordered search, pruning by maximality).
- Provides an implementation and empirical evaluation demonstrating significant improvements over ELPS on the tested instances.
- Includes a proof sketch/appendix establishing equivalence with Shen & Eiter semantics and argues correctness of the partitioning approach.
- Exploits bitvector encoding and pragmatic design choices to make the implementation efficient; supports parallel solving and early termination.

Weaknesses and concerns / Suggestions for improvement:
- Experimental evaluation is limited in scope: only two problem families (eligibleN and yaleN) are shown, and comparison is limited to ELPS. The paper should include comparisons with other available ELP tools (e.g., ESmodels, Wviews) or justify their omission, and use a broader benchmark suite to better assess generality.
- Experimental details are sparse: parameter settings (nG, np), grounding/translation choices, and variability across runs (e.g., multiple runs, variance) are not fully reported. More detailed ablation studies (effect of nG, grouping strategy, pruning on/off, parallel scaling) would strengthen claims.
- Some heuristics (e.g., those for the conformant planning module) are mentioned but not described in depth; discussion of how generalizable they are and when they apply would be useful.
- Reproducibility: no information on code availability; releasing the solver and exact input encodings would improve impact and adoption.
- While correctness is argued, parts of the proof rely on prior translations and transformations; a formal complexity discussion (worst-case behavior, memory bounds) would be helpful.

Recommendation:
I recommend this paper for dissemination. With an expanded experimental section, clearer reproduction artifacts, and more discussion of limitations and parameter choices, it would make a strong contribution to the ASP/ELP community.
